skill.name: figure-reference-checker
skill.description: Check consistency of figure references in academic manuscripts by detecting orphaned references, inconsistent labels, and missing citations.
skill.summary: Validates figure reference consistency in academic manuscripts
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Evaluated all 44 static analysis detections and confirmed every one is a false positive. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' matches are spurious pattern collisions on plain text. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' detections are all markdown code fences in documentation. The 'path traversal' detections are from a security checklist warning against '../' usage. The 'hardcoded URL' is a standard GitHub source attribution link. The underlying Python script is a benign 38-line argparse+regex tool with no network access, no file I/O, no eval, and no command execution. One minor concern: the --manuscript parameter accepts unbounded text input without size limits.

low_findings.title: Unbounded Text Input via --manuscript Parameter
low_findings.description: The scripts/main.py argparse argument --manuscript accepts arbitrary-length string input with no size validation. While the regex pattern used (r'Fig\.?\s*(\d+)[A-Z]?') is not vulnerable to ReDoS, very large inputs could cause resource exhaustion during regex matching.

low_findings.title: Ambiguous Parameter Description
low_findings.description: The --manuscript argument help text says 'Manuscript text or file' but the implementation treats the value as raw text for regex matching, not as a file path to open. This documentation ambiguity could lead users to expect file reading behavior that does not exist.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Detected as Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged backtick characters in markdown code blocks (```bash ... ```) as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. These are documentation examples showing how to run the Python script, not executable backtick commands. Found in SKILL.md and references/audit-reference.md.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Security Checklist Detected as Path Traversal
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged '../' sequences on SKILL.md lines 118 and 121 as path traversal. These lines are part of a security checklist (lines 117-125) that explicitly warns against path traversal: 'No unauthorized file system access (../)' and 'Input file paths validated (no ../ traversal)'. The text is documenting a defensive measure, not performing path traversal.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Pattern Collisions
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer reported 'weak cryptographic algorithm' at multiple locations across all four files. None of the files contain any cryptographic code. The Python script only uses argparse and re modules. The SKILL.md and audit-reference.md are plain markdown. The audit_result_v2.json is evaluation data. These are spurious pattern collisions with no security relevance.

content.user_title: Check Figure References in Academic Manuscripts
content.value_statement: Academic manuscripts often contain figure reference errors such as mislabeled figures or orphaned citations that slip past manual review. This skill automates the detection of these inconsistencies to improve manuscript quality before journal submission.
actual_capabilities: Detects figure references in manuscript text using regex pattern matching
Identifies orphaned figure references that have no corresponding figure in the document
Checks consistency of figure label formatting across the manuscript
Produces a sorted list of all figure references found in the input text
Provides a structured output format with explicit assumptions and limitations
Supports both inline text input and document-based workflows
limitations: Only matches the pattern Fig./Fig optionally followed by digits and an optional letter suffix
Does not parse actual figure files or images, only matches text references
Cannot verify if a referenced figure actually exists in the document beyond text matching
Only works with English figure reference formats and may miss non-standard labeling schemes

use_cases.title: Pre-submission Manuscript Review
use_cases.description: A researcher checks their manuscript for figure reference errors before submitting to a journal. The skill scans the text and lists all detected figure references for manual verification against the actual figures.

use_cases.title: Editorial Quality Control
use_cases.description: A journal editor or peer reviewer uses the skill to quickly verify that all figure references in a submitted manuscript are consistent and properly formatted, flagging potential issues early in the review process.

use_cases.title: Collaborative Writing Pipeline
use_cases.description: A research team integrates the skill into their collaborative writing workflow to automatically check figure references each time the manuscript is updated, catching inconsistencies introduced during multi-author edits.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Figure Reference Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Check the figure references in my manuscript. The text is: 'As shown in Fig. 1, the results demonstrate... Fig. 2 provides additional evidence. Refer to Fig. 3A for the control group data.'
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: Quick check of figure references in a short text passage

prompt_templates.title: Full Manuscript Validation
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the figure reference checker on my manuscript file at /path/to/manuscript.docx. I need a complete list of all figure references detected, sorted by figure number, along with any inconsistencies or orphaned references found.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: Complete manuscript scan with structured output

prompt_templates.title: Comparative Reference Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the figure references in the introduction section against the figures actually included in the manuscript. The introduction mentions Fig. 1, Fig. 2B, and Fig. 4, but I only have figures 1 through 3. Identify any references to missing figures.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: Cross-referencing text mentions against actual figures

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Section Consistency Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Check figure reference consistency across all sections of my manuscript. I need to verify that figures are referenced in sequential order, that no figure number is skipped in the text, and that supplementary figures are clearly distinguished from main figures.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: Cross-section consistency validation for complex manuscripts

output_examples.input: Check figure references in: 'Fig. 1 shows the methodology. Results are presented in Fig. 2 and Fig. 3A. Supplemental data appears in Fig. S1.'
output: Assumptions: Text treated as plain string input. Only Fig./Fig patterns matched.
Risks: Cannot verify actual figure existence. Supplementary figure 'S1' detected but format may vary.

output_examples.input: Validate manuscript section with references to Fig. 1, Fig. 3, and Fig. 4 but only Fig. 1 and Fig. 3 exist as actual figures.
output: Warning: Fig. 4 is referenced in text but may not have a corresponding figure.
Recommendation: Verify Fig. 4 exists or update reference to next available figure number.

best_practices: Always provide the complete manuscript text or section as input rather than partial excerpts for accurate detection
Review the sorted output list against your actual figure files to confirm each reference has a matching figure
Run the checker after each major manuscript revision to catch newly introduced reference errors
anti_patterns: Do not rely solely on the skill output without manually verifying figure existence and correct labeling
Avoid using the skill on non-English manuscripts without first confirming figure reference format conventions
Do not pass file paths expecting automatic file reading when the script processes the argument as raw text

faq.question: What figure reference formats does this skill detect?
faq.answer: The skill uses the regex pattern Fig. or Fig followed by optional space, digits, and an optional letter suffix. It detects formats like Fig. 1, Fig 2, Fig. 3A, and Fig.4B.

faq.question: Can this skill verify that figures actually exist in my document?
faq.answer: No. The skill only matches text references in the manuscript. It cannot parse image files or embedded figures. You must manually verify that each detected reference has a corresponding figure.

faq.question: Does this skill modify my manuscript files?
faq.answer: No. The skill only reads input text and reports findings. It does not edit, modify, or rewrite any manuscript files.

faq.question: What Python version is required to run the script?
faq.answer: Python 3.10 or later is required. The script uses only standard library modules (argparse and re) with no external dependencies.

faq.question: Can this skill handle multi-panel figures labeled as Fig. 1A, Fig. 1B?
faq.answer: Yes. The regex pattern includes an optional letter suffix, so multi-panel labels like Fig. 1A, Fig. 1B, and Fig. 2C are all detected correctly.

faq.question: What should I do if the skill reports an error?
faq.answer: Check that you provided the --manuscript argument with text content. If execution fails, review the error message, confirm Python 3.10+ is installed, and verify the script file exists at scripts/main.py.
